description: Humid tropical zone with > 1200mm annual rainfall and no prolonged dry season. Dominant farming systems: tree crops, mixed rainfed, root crops. Agroforestry widely practised (cocoa, coffee, home gardens). Primary slope constraint is more relaxed due to high biomass and erosion control provided by tree cover.

Sub-humid tropical zone with 800–1200mm annual rainfall and a defined dry season (3–5 months). Dominant farming systems: maize-mixed, mixed rainfed, agropastoral. Classic zone for agroforestry with leguminous trees (Gliricidia, Leucaena, Faidherbia). Drought is a seasonal constraint requiring careful species selection.

Semi-arid zone with 300–800mm annual rainfall and 5–8 month dry season. Aridity index 0.2–0.5. Dominant systems: agropastoral millet/sorghum, pastoral. Parkland agroforestry (Faidherbia albida, Vitellaria paradoxa) and FMNR are the appropriate sub-types. Drought is the primary constraint; rainfall threshold overrides are required. Haile (2024) and Ahmad et al. (2020) demonstrate viable agroforestry in this zone.

Highland tropical zone at > 1500m elevation with cooler temperatures (mean annual temp 10–18°C) and typically 800–1500mm annual rainfall. Dominant systems: highland mixed, root crops, tea/coffee. Slope constraints are more relaxed for agroforestry due to traditional terracing practices. Nath et al. (2021) and Mushtaq et al. (2023) cover this zone in the Eastern Himalayas and Kashmir respectively.

Temperate European zone with 500–900mm annual rainfall and seasonal temperature variation. Mediterranean and Atlantic climate subtypes. Dominant systems: cereal-based mixed farming, permanent crops, silvopasture. Palma et al. (2007) and Kiziridis et al. (2026) cover silvoarable and silvopastoral systems in this zone. Temperature lower bounds apply for frost-sensitive species.

Sub-humid tropical zone specific to sub-Saharan Africa context where road infrastructure is sparse and market access constraints require more relaxed suitability thresholds. Chuma et al. (2021) DRC and Ahmad & Goparaju (2017) India studies apply here. Road access distance override is wider than global default. Farming systems: mixed rainfed, agropastoral, tree crop.

Hyperarid and arid zones with < 250mm annual rainfall and aridity index < 0.2. WH is among the very few viable agricultural NbS. Dominant sub-practices: wadi-based check dams, cisterns, micro-catchments, Vallerani technique. Evaporation losses are high and must be factored into storage sizing. Primary context for Iraq, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen papers in the stocktake (Adham et al. 2018, Hassan et al. 2025, Al-Shabeeb et al. 2022, Al-Shamiri & Ziadat 2012).

context_name: Middle East and North Africa dryland (MENA arid)
description: MENA-specific arid context characterised by wadi systems, limestone geology, and traditional RWH heritage (cisterns, spate irrigation). The largest geographic cluster in the WH stocktake: Iraq (Adham 2018, Hassan 2025, multiple others), Jordan (Al-Shabeeb 2022, Alrawi 2023), Palestine (Shadeed 2019), Yemen (Al-Shamiri 2012), Saudi Arabia. Wadi stream-order suitability applies; geology/lineament density is a key additional input variable used by MENA papers but absent in global default T4.

South Asian semi-arid context covering Deccan Plateau, Rajasthan, and Pakistan dryland zones. Government subsidy schemes (MGNREGS) substantially reduce effective smallholder WH costs. Check dams and percolation tanks are the dominant government-promoted sub-practices. Key papers: Singh et al. (2016), Vema et al. (2019), Jha et al. (2014), Zheng et al. (2023) Pakistan.

context_name: East Africa semi-arid and sub-humid (Ethiopia, Tanzania, Rwanda)
description: East African dryland context spanning Ethiopia, Tanzania, Kenya, and Rwanda. The second-largest geographic cluster in the WH stocktake. Strong government and NGO support for WH through IWMI, CGIAR, and national programmes. Key papers: Alene et al. (2022), Bera equivalent in Ethiopia, Mahmoud et al. (2014), Grum et al. (2016), Mbilinyi et al. (2007), Umugwaneza et al. (2022), Amdihun et al. (2014), Worku et al. (2020).
Sierra Leone national boundary (agroforestry pilot AOI).
EO-derived: rainfed cropland (cropland + no significant irrigation + low tree cover + low livestock density). Default for rainfed staple systems.
EO-derived: cropland + GMIA irrigation share above threshold. Paddy, irrigated horticulture, irrigation schemes.
EO-derived: cropland co-located with moderate-to-high livestock density (GLW). Mixed smallholder systems incl. highland-mixed, maize-mixed.
EO-derived: sparse cropland + grazing-dominant land cover + livestock-dominant economy. Sahelian millet/sorghum + livestock contexts.
EO-derived: grassland/shrubland-dominant with high livestock density and minimal cropland. Pastoralist systems.
EO-derived: tree-perennial-crop dominant (cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, fruit) — Hansen tree cover + MapSPAM perennial mask.
